title: Micro-Frontends
summary: Micro-frontends split a large app into smaller, independent pieces.
Different teams can build and deploy each piece separately.
It helps very large projects scale.
It adds complexity, so it is not for small apps.

sections.text: For very large applications built by many teams, one giant codebase can become a bottleneck. The micro-frontend approach breaks the app into smaller, independent pieces that teams can build and ship on their own.

sections.title: What Micro-Frontends Are
sections.text: A micro-frontend architecture splits one big frontend into several smaller apps, each owned by a different team. Each piece can be developed, tested, and deployed independently, then combined into the full experience the user sees. It is the frontend version of breaking a large system into smaller services.

sections.title: The Trade Off
sections.text: The benefit is that big teams can work in parallel without stepping on each other, and ship on their own schedules. The cost is real added complexity: combining the pieces smoothly, keeping them consistent, and avoiding duplicated work all take effort. So micro-frontends suit large organizations, not small projects, where they would be overkill.

media.alt: One large frontend split into independent team owned pieces

sections.title: A Food Court vs One Kitchen
sections.text: A normal app is like one big kitchen serving everything. Micro-frontends are like a food court, where each stall is run independently and specializes, all under one roof for the visitor. Great for a large venue with many vendors, but pointless and wasteful if you are just running a single small cafe.

media.alt: A food court of independent stalls compared to one small kitchen

sections.title: Considering Micro-Frontends
items: Notice a very large app and many teams.
Split the frontend into independent pieces.
Let each team own and deploy its piece.
Combine the pieces into one experience.
Weigh the added complexity, and skip it for small apps.

sections.text: Micro-frontends borrow their idea from microservices on the backend, where a large system is split into small independent services. The same scaling instinct, applied to the frontend.

terms.definition: A large frontend split into independent pieces.
Built and shipped on its own.
The added cost that makes this unfit for small apps.

options: Split a large app into smaller, independent pieces
questions.explanation: Micro-frontends split a large app into smaller, independent pieces.

questions.text: What can teams do with them?
options: Build and deploy each piece separately
questions.explanation: Different teams can build and deploy each piece separately.

options: No, they add complexity and suit large projects
questions.explanation: Micro-frontends add complexity, so they suit large projects, not small apps.
